data.title: Why I'm Betting 20% Of My Portfolio On LandBridge's Future
data.description:
Introduction

It’s time to talk about my single-biggest investment, LandBridge Company (LB), which isn’t just important because of its 20% weighting in my portfolio, but also because it perfectly captures a wide range of my bullish theses, including these major themes:

  • Rising oil and gas prices.
  • The booming produced water business in the Permian Basin.
  • Increasing commercial surface value in the Permian.
  • Related to the point above, the AI data center boom is shifting its focus to West Texas.

However, as bullish as I may have been since my first article was written a few days after its IPO (the first public article on LB on the entire internet), this has not been an easy ride so far.

Since it went public, it has been through many ups and downs, caused by factors like the oil price decline, causing uncertainty with regard to the future of the Permian and related water volumes, as well as fears that LandBridge may "never" get a big data center deal.

Nonetheless, as most readers know, I have used every major drawdown to add more stock, writing very bullish articles like the one titled "I Have Never Been More Bullish On LandBridge" after it got hit hard after 2Q25 earnings. Since then, shares are up 50%.

See, LandBridge may be a landowner in the Permian, where it owns roughly 300,000 surface acres in the heart of the Delaware Basin, but it’s very different from Viper Energy (VNOM) and even Texas Pacific Land (TPL). Viper Energy makes 100% of its money from sub-surface mineral royalties. Texas Pacific gets half of its revenues from these operations.

LandBridge was initially founded to manage the land of WaterBridge Infrastructure (WBI), which went public this year. WaterBridge is the biggest water service provider in the Permian Basin, where it owns a huge network of pipelines and processing assets that help oil and gas producers deal with an increasing water problem.

This is produced water, which is a byproduct of oil and gas production. In many areas of the Delaware Basin, which has the deepest reserve of the Permian, every barrel of oil comes with up to ten (or even more) barrels of toxic water that needs to be dealt with.

There’s so much water in the Delaware that some even call it a water basin. The chart below is from WaterBridge, which shows that falling disposal capacity is meeting accelerating produced water volumes. This bodes very well for pricing.

LandBridge has land (pore space), which is used to get rid of water. It also has surface land used to establish large water facilities. Even better, as it has land in an inverse L-shape around the Texas/New Mexico border (see the map above), it has the best assets to benefit from water flows in the areas with the best reserves.

Please feel free to read my in-depth article on water, written on July 9.

This explains why oil and gas royalties account for just 7% of their revenues on a year-to-date basis. Roughly two-thirds of its revenue comes from surface operations, which includes its bigger focus on "powered land," as it knows that having remote land with extremely cheap power (Waha natural gas is way cheaper than Henry Hub), plenty of potential cooling water, and other benefits, can attract major commercial players, like solar farm operators, crypto miners, and, eventually massive data centers.

I have written in countless articles that I am convinced that next-gen data centers cannot be built close to large population centers. For cloud data centers, that wasn’t an issue, as they had computing power of a few hundred MW. That’s a lot, but the new AI-focused data centers require multiple GW. This requires as much power as entire major cities, enormous amounts of cooling water, and a lot of space away from stakeholders like residential areas.

Over the past 24 months, I would say that the thesis was muted. Nobody really cared, as no announcements were made in West Texas. Now, that is changing, as Texas Pacific Land confirmed in its 3Q25 earnings call:

Look, we feel like TPL is very well positioned. We have all of the attributes needed to be very attractive to power generators and data center developers, hyperscalers. I think we've probably got more available land with those attributes needed than anyone else in West Texas, and I think West Texas is quickly becoming more and more popular as an area to build out multi-gig facilities and campuses. I would just say that we're -- we continue to have really good conversations. - TPL 3Q25

This explained why LandBridge’s stock price rallied so hard from its lows. While the oil business remains a hot mess (in general) due to subdued prices, the market has woken up to the massive potential that data centers could provide. Once oil rebounds, I think we’ll see a perfect tailwind for LandBridge.

In its just-released earnings report, the company confirmed all of that.

So, let me update you on my highest-conviction investment.

LandBridge Delivers On Everything I Care About

LandBridge believes it has at least three major advantages when it comes to its 300,000 acres:

  • Most of its land is contiguous, which means it is protected against the elevated pore pressure challenges in other areas. In other words, the company has prime real estate for produced water disposal, as it escapes some of the biggest challenges like declining pore space availability.
  • It has a partnership with WaterBridge, as I already mentioned. They even share a management team.
  • It has regulatory tailwinds. This includes the Texas Railroad Commission, which has a strong focus on avoiding over-pressurization in the Permian. After all, too much pressure can cause earthquakes. As we can see below, its assets are in low-pressure areas, which means we will likely see more pipelines in the future that ship millions of barrels of water to LandBridge’s land.

Hence, these comments may not come as a surprise:

We continue to unlock new opportunities with leading developers across energy, infrastructure and environmental sectors, creating diverse and resilient cash flow streams [...]. - LB 3Q25

That’s why LandBridge was originally founded, to provide the pore space for WaterBridge (and third-party players) to deal with what I consider the biggest bottleneck problem in American energy. By the end of 2035, the company sees a 9-million-barrel-per-day shortfall. That’s a huge deal and great news for pipeline demand and pore space pricing.

M&A is a big part of this strategy, as LandBridge had no land in 2019. When it went public, it had 220,000 acres. Now, it’s 300,000. As we can clearly see below, the company is essentially building a wall of land around New Mexico, "forcing" companies in the best drilling spots to use its land.

Its most recent deal is the acquisition of the 1918 Ranch, which consists of 37,500 acres in Loving, Reeves, Winkler, and Ward counties. There’s room for more than 25 produced water handling facilities. Next year, this deal is expected to add $20 million in EBITDA, which means it paid 12.5x EBITDA for the property. Historically, the company has reduced this to 3-4x. In this case, management sees a path to $50 million in EBITDA due to 900,000 barrels per day in additional pore space.

While some bearish people may argue that LB buys land to make its revenue growth look better, the company buys land when it sees long-term commercialization opportunities. After all, this is about offering options that other landowners cannot offer. Specifically, the land is contiguous, as we already covered. This means large deals can happen without the approval of other stakeholders.

This includes a number of deals:

  • A 3,000-acre solar deal for 250 MW in capacity. Although the company didn’t share the name of the developer, I think this is a major step toward providing more power for commercial usage in the Permian.
  • The company is working with ONEOK (OKE), as it closed the deal for a long-term lease for natural gas processing facilities.
  • In September, the company agreed to a strategic deal with NRG Energy (NRG), which allows this power provider to potentially build a 1.1 GW natural gas power plant on its land. One doesn’t need a lot of imagination to guess that this may be for future data centers.

It’s also reflected in its numbers, as total EBITDA grew by 79%, supported by 78% higher revenues. It ended up with an 88% adjusted EBITDA margin and a 66% free cash flow margin, which means $0.66 of every revenue dollar ended up as free cash flow. This is a phenomenal number supported by its asset-light business model, as all it does is let others use its land. Even better, just 7% of its revenue came from oil and gas royalties.

Moreover, despite new M&A, the company lowered its net debt to 2.2x.

As LandBridge targets a net leverage ratio of 2.0x to 2.5x, it means new M&A is likely (assuming it finds new opportunities). After all, its main capital spending priority is M&A. Priority number two is maintaining a strong balance sheet. The third one is returning capital. However, as its dividend yields less than 1%, that’s not really worth mentioning for at least a few more years, I think.

This brings me to the next part.

Data Centers & Valuation

While I am writing this, LB is down 12% after earnings. This is becoming a common occurrence, as it was down 17% after its 2Q25 earnings. However, since then, it is up roughly 50%, as I already noted, breaking a new all-time high before reporting earnings.

This is common because Wall Street has a hard time judging LandBridge. To many, it’s a data center player without a data center. To me, it’s a water-focused energy company with an increasing focus on surface operations. Even if they never get a data center, I would be very bullish on the company. Bear in mind that I don’t run an event-driven portfolio. I am a long-term investor focusing on the long end of the Equity Yield Curve (see this article).

I invest in hidden opportunities.

During its prepared earnings call remarks, the company did not even mention data centers specifically. When that happened, I knew LB would sell off.

The thing is that LB cannot discuss these opportunities unless it has a signed agreement. If it had said something along the lines of "We expect to sign at least two data centers over the next 2-3 years," it would have potentially created false hopes. It would be legally irresponsible to get investors' hopes up.

Moreover, as I have often written, building data centers takes time. We are still in the early innings of the buildout. It could easily take another 1-2 years until we get a data center fact.

The good news is that the company was asked by analysts to reveal new updates. I believe these comments were even more bullish compared to what Texas Pacific said, as

I mean, the economic fundamentals of West Texas just made that inevitable. And as I've said before, it was always a win, [not a discussion], and we're starting to see those come to fruition here.

I mean, from our seat, we are further along into existing conversations and also engaged with a number of new blue-chip counterparties in these discussions. And so we're very excited and very optimistic about the progress we're making, and we look forward to sharing new milestones when the time is right.

Moreover, the market is now finding out that West Texas is an obvious place for data centers, as it has plenty of land, cheap energy, an unregulated grid, qualified labor, and nearly unlimited cooling water. Hence, over the past 6-12 months, LandBridge has seen a shift in engagement. Hence, it is currently talking to more than one player. - LB 3Q25

And, as we’re seeing, there’s an entire ecosystem under construction in the region of renewables and natural gas plants, transmission projects, generators, and more demand for water cleaning technologies (that’s great for WaterBridge).

As counterparties require simplicity, LandBridge has an edge over any other major player, including Texas Pacific Land, which has mostly checkerboard-like land.

Moreover, FivePoint, which is the majority shareholder of LandBridge, is still in talks with Commonwealth Asset Management, which is the company looking to build a data center. They are still working on that. While LandBridge cannot reveal more, it means nothing has been canceled, which increases the likelihood that LandBridge will host at least one major data center. Note that it’s a 2-year option period. We’re in the second year.

Note that even one deal would create unprecedented growth due to:

  • Long-term leases with inflation escalators.
  • High upfront payments.
  • Power-margin sharing (LB gets a share of the power costs).
  • Water volume margins.
  • Massive easement demand.
  • Demand for related power infrastructure.

But then again, even without data centers, I like LB.

Currently, LB has a $5.4 billion market cap. When adding $340 million in net debt, we get an enterprise value of roughly $5.7 billion. The company stuck to its guidance of $165 million to $175 million in adjusted EBITDA. This would imply a 33x multiple (midpoint).

Texas Pacific Land, the only comparable stock, has traded between 20x to 25x EBITDA for more than 25 years. I believe 25-30x EBITDA is fair for LB, as we are dealing with an ultra-high-margin player that has no CapEx requirements to benefit from secular growth in water and surface commercial use. It also has a healthy balance sheet.

In other words, the current price is very fair if we exclude any growth.

Applying a "quick and dirty" approach, I see more than $200 million in EBITDA next year, assuming 8% organic growth in its core business and $20 million from the 1918 Ranch. Through 2028, that number could rise to at least $240 million based on the very conservative 8% organic growth rate. This excludes any M&A, any major pricing benefits, and any major upswing in drilling activity.

Also, it excludes any data center tailwinds.

This would imply a $7.9 billion enterprise value. Assuming no major net debt changes, it would imply a 40% upside. Again, 33x EBITDA may be high for some, but I am also using conservative growth rates here.

A more bullish outlook would see 12% annual organic growth, at least $10 million in annual benefits from commercial operations (i.e., solar projects), and stronger water pricing. This would imply at least $300 million in EBITDA, or nearly 80% upside to a 124 price target.

Essentially, that’s why I always say this is at least a $120 stock without any data centers.

I also worked on modeling data center benefits, but given the complexity, I am waiting for that until I have more data. After all, I do not want to overpromise anything, either.

Closing Thoughts (My Takeaway)

LandBridge is a volatile beast. While it’s my favorite holding, I have cursed it a number of times. Right now, it’s selling off once again. Although its happening after a huge rally, it’s still frustrating, as it seems the market has no clue how to value LandBridge.

While I could be wrong (the market may be right), I see a business with a business model that combines the best of multiple worlds, including energy drilling, produced water, and commercial surface operations.

The company continues to shine, as it executes new deals while protecting its balance sheet, paving the way for what I expect to be at least $300 million in 2028 EBITDA.

When it comes to the short term, I have no idea what will happen. As long as oil trades below $70, I expect the market to refrain from panic buying LandBridge, as higher prices are needed to get drillers excited about the future again.

In the meantime, I expect the water business to grow and LB to work with major players on a potential data center deal. It may happen sooner than later. It’s hard to tell.

Either way, as long as this stock trades below $120, I’m feeling good about buying dips.

Risks To My Thesis

LandBridge is volatile. If you dislike that, please stay away. It may never get a data center deal, either. While I don’t need a deal to make my thesis work, it could keep euphoria subdued, as the market has turned LB into an AI data center stock.

Also, prolonged oil prices below $70 will keep energy-related surface growth limited.

data.title: Amazon: The Best Opportunity Out Of The Magnificent 7?
data.description:
Introduction

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) is one of the most structurally diversified large technology companies in the world.

In this analysis, we take a look at why Amazon is a promising investment and could be a core building block in your long-term portfolio, as it is potentially the most attractive Magnificent 7 stock out there. Meta Platforms (META) was second in line for me, but then there was the news that Yann LeCun, who is Meta's Chief AI Scientist, is likely leaving to build his own start-up, which is, in a clear AI race, not the best sign.

As can be seen, the stocks moved in opposite directions this month.

Amazon is a company with a balanced ecosystem across e-commerce, cloud, computing, advertising, and other subscription services. Over the years, Amazon has proven to have consistent operating leverage even in more difficult economic environments.

Today’s environment is one of those more difficult periods; short-term pressures on free cash flow and valuation have shown that there is some skepticism around Amazon and its MOAT.

However, these are the consequences of a decent load of front-loaded investments, which will only pay off down the road.

In this article, we will discuss the current headwinds, why we believe Amazon will continue to thrive in the upcoming years. Furthermore, we will discuss the latest quarterly earnings and discuss the risks and opportunities with this thesis.

Last but not least, we will take a look at why Amazon could be one of the building blocks of your portfolio.

What Does Amazon Do and Its Ecosystem

Amazon has three primary business segments: North America, International, and Amazon Web Services, known as AWS.

North America contributes to Amazon’s revenue through sales of Amazon Prime, subscriptions, and advertising services across the United States, Canada, and Mexico.

When we take a look at the International segment, Amazon operations are held in Europe, Japan, and emerging markets that are continuing to scale, but generating a smaller profit share due to currency and coordination costs.

Amazon Web Services ("AWS") is the biggest profit engine, providing global cloud, infrastructure, data storage, AI chips, and enterprise solutions.

In FY2024, Amazon generated revenue of 638 billion (+11% YoY) and operating income of around 68 billion. AWS accounted for less than 20% of total sales but generated 58% of operating profit.

Revenue streams of Amazon are diversified across first-party, retail third-party, marketplace fees, Prime memberships, advertising, and AWS services. More than 60% of items sold come from an independent seller. Making Amazon generate strong fee-based revenues.

Investment Thesis

Amazon positions itself as one of the most strategically diversified and operationally efficient companies in the global market. Especially given its huge size, it is remarkable how efficient they are.

Despite the near-term cash flow compression and high expenditures, it is a long-term path to high-growth margins and profitability.

The company’s premium compared to peers such as Alibaba (BABA) and PDD Holdings (PDD) appears justifiable given its revenue mix and operating business lines, and of course, the premium due to the China risk that always exists with the others. Although, I believe this is often significantly overstated.

Amazon is no longer just an e-commerce company. It runs in an ecosystem where over 40% of profits are stamped from segments such as AWS, advertising, and third-party marketplace services.

I personally see AI and Amazon Web Services as one of the biggest catalysts in the company’s growth. The latest Capital Expenditure related to AI chip development, Anthropic (ANTHRO) partnership integration, would be able to support sustained double-digit AWS growth recovery within the next couple of years.

Valuation & Peer Comparison

At roughly 32x FWD P/E and 3.3x EV/Sales, Amazon currently trades at a premium to its e-commerce peers, including Alibaba at 22.7x FWD P/E , and 2.47x EV/Sales.

Also compared to PDD, which trades at 13.2x FWD P/E and a 2.16x EV/Sales, Amazon is trading at a premium.

For reference, all the metrics discussed can be found in the table at the end of this paragraph. Numbers are slightly outdated as the market has moved a bit in the last week, while I was working on this article.

Amazon’s Revenue multiple shows a moderate premium compared to peers. A relative premium is justifiable for Amazon. The premium compared to Chinese peers is because both Alibaba and PDD are highly exposed to macroeconomic and geopolitical changes. These are risks that Amazon is to a lesser extent exposed.

On the contrary, MercadoLibre’s (MELI) valuation is near 46x earnings, which reflects its pure exposure to Latin American market growth, rather than the operational diversification that Amazon provides. While MercadoLibre trades at 46x FWD P/E, it has a narrower geographic scope, which makes the company more vulnerable and cyclical across the macro environment. I really like MercadoLibre, by the way; you can find my latest analysis on the company here.

Amazon’s premium compared to its Chinese peers is based on a balanced mix of cloud, advertising, and third-party marketplace revenue, all of which carry structurally higher margins than retail.

The high Price-to-Book ratio of 6.8 exceeds its peers, which is mainly because the book value ignores Amazon’s vast intangible assets—the Amazon Web Service infrastructure, proprietary algorithms, and the fulfillment network—which give Amazon durable, competitive advantages and a high incremental ROIC.

Therefore, Amazon may appear elevated on the surface. Yet, the business quality, profitability profile, and long-term free cash flow potential might justify at least part of a premium compared to traditional e-commerce peers. The market positions Amazon between a retail platform and a diversified technology provider.

Important to note is that on a forward P/CF of 15.8x, Amazon trades below Alibaba, showing that the free cash flow is compressed. While Amazon’s valuation appears elevated compared to Chinese peers, it is still discounted compared to MercadoLibre.

I believe that the market has not fully priced in the company’s medium-term free-cash-flow rebound expected from AWS and normalization of CapEx.

Huge Opportunity for Sustainable Growth

Free cash flow declined from 53 billion to 18 billion as CapEx nearly doubled. The compression of free cash flow, however, stems primarily from loaded investments in technology infrastructure, AI data center capacity, and logistic optimization.

These all reflect that the compression is not the effect of weakening fundamentals.

Operating cash flow increased by $13 billion YoY, and CapEx surged from $64 billion to $107 billion. Depreciation and amortization expenses rose after the useful life of services was shortened from 6 years to 5. This increased depreciation by $280 million and reduced income by $217 million. This was reflected by a $0.02 decrease in EPS. Important to note is that the cash position didn't decrease, so these non-cash accounting effects temporarily lower earnings without affecting underlying cash.

With 31% YoY growth and an operating income growing 52% YoY. Amazon’s valuation multiples barely moved, showing that the market focuses on near-term cash flow, which overshadows Amazon’s continuously improving fundamentals.

As shown historically by Amazon, every major CapEx surge was followed by a 1.5-2x rebound in free cash flow within two years. This cycle may follow a similar path.

Amazon entered the 2nd half of 2025 with one of the strongest operational setups in its history. Still, the company continues to compound. AWS net sales grew by 18% YoY in Q2 2025, keeping double-digit momentum, despite slower global IT spending. AWS' backlog reached 995 billion with an average 4-year contract duration, providing multiyear visibility into future cash flow.

By late 2025-2026, AWS is expected to experience earnings inflation as AI workload, monetization, and depreciation normalize. With this, cash flow may rebound as operating leverage will re-expand.

Q3 Earnings Surpassed All Expectations, With AWS on Top

Amazon crushed its quarterly results and highlighted its dominance across e-commerce and cloud infrastructure.

Q3 total net sales increased 13% YoY to $180.2B compared to $158.9 billion in 2024, and nearly $3 billion above analysts’ expectations. EPS was up 16% to $1.95 vs. the estimated $1.58.

AWS once again proved to be the key revenue driver for the company, with sales exceeding $33 billion (+20% YoY). Another significantly growing sector is advertising, which grew to $17.7B and exceeded the consensus of $17.34B.

Amazon’s online store sales were up 10% ($67.41B vs. expected $66.93B), and 3rd party seller services were 12% up ($42.5B vs. $42.05B). North America segment sales rose 11% YoY to $106.27B vs. $104.96B expected. The International segment sales grew 14% YoY to $40.9B, surpassing the consensus of $40.77B.

The weaker part of earnings was the operating income, which was held nearly flat at $17.42 billion, and was missing consensus. The operating margin lowered 130 basis points to 9.7%, vs. 11% expected. North America’s operating margin declined to 4.5%, and internationally, margins fell to 9.7%. But that is due to there being two one-time charges: $2.5B related to a legal settlement with the Federal Trade Commission, and another one of $1.8B in estimated severance costs.

Operating cash flow increased 16%, reaching $130.7 billion, compared to $112.7 billion on Sept. 30, 2024. The number is important, as investors want to see whether Amazon can generate sufficient cash flow to support large CAPEX on Artificial Intelligence.

Free cash flow decreased to $14.8B vs. $47.7B in Q3 2024, mostly due to the purchase of property and equipment of $50.9B.

Amazon’s management says AWS is growing at a pace they have not seen since 2022, accelerating to 20.2% YoY.

Net sales are expected to be around $206B and $213B, growth of between 10% and 13% compared with Q4 2024. Operating income is estimated at $21B-$26B, compared with $21.2B in Q4 2024.

Overall, Amazon delivered durable growth results with AWS, and advertising was the company’s most notable revenue driver. Shares surged more than 10% in after-hours trading following the Q3 Earnings Release.

What Are the Risks, and Are They Overstated?

Everything seems perfect, but what are the risks? Amazon’s expansion into new product services regions exposes it to several measurable risks.

  1. Heavy CapEx and free-cash-flow compression. With FCF falling from 53 billion to 18 billion. Expenditure has already grown by 2X, and as deployment completes, the growing expenditures convert revenue capacity. It has already been evident from Amazon’s history after each significant capital expenditure wave, like 2014/2019/2021. Afterward, FCF rebounded 1.5-2 times within the next two years. I believe that this cycle may turn out the same way once again. However, there is a big caveat with AI spending, as I am not sure this will provide the productivity gains in the short term to justify the heavy CapEx. The CapEx amount is more concentrated on AI data centers, cost chips, and logistic automation that are all monetized capacity with long payback tails.

  2. AWS margin and demand volatility is a second risk. AWS is the major profit engine. In Q2 2025, it reported operating income of 10.2 billion. Still, Amazon operates in an incredibly competitive environment, where Microsoft Azure (MSFT) and Google Cloud (GOOGL) compete aggressively on AI-shaped models and tooling. Price/Discount wars between companies can pressure gross margins. And this will show up with a clear margin of compression, even when the revenue is fine. As AI workloads take time to scale, this also means slower expansion of operating margin. Right now, it all depends on how Amazon can monetize the speed of AI workload and pricing discipline. However, if Microsoft Azure and Google are able to play the pricing war, this could cause further compression.

  3. Foreign exchange and macro-economic risks. Due to Amazon's vast global operations, they are also exposed to FX swings and different consumer cycles. Overall, a large adverse/positive FX move can cause large other income/expense fluctuations. The cash conversion can worsen if the receivable cycle lengthens or the payables shorten. However, it would be better to keep in mind that Amazon is vastly diversified, and nearly 40% of earnings come from AWS, ads, and subscriptions. Amazon can take care of demand and concentrate on cash flow compared to some of its peers. Strengthening in the USD can also hurt its earnings. On the flip side, a weaker USD could lift it.

  4. Regulatory and legal exposure. Amazon has an ongoing investigation into U.S. and EU marketplace practices, third-party seller treatments, and data usage. For example, in June 2025, Xockets Inc. filed two patent infringement complaints related to the AWS Nitro System. However, given the balance sheet of Amazon with $61.5 billion in cash and no material covenants, Amazon can handle fines and settlements without a problem. Still, I recommend monitoring any other direct constraints that might have an even larger effect.

Conclusion

Staying cautious, risks regarding capital expenditure on AWS and competition with Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud play a moderate role in Amazon’s business environment.

The macroeconomic risks and others mentioned are the real ones and moderate. Still, most of them look transitional, short-term, and tied to reinvestment timing and accounting. It surely isn't due to a weakening of the company’s fundamentals.

While looking ahead, if the high capital expenditure cycle ends and FCF rebounds with high ROIC and balance sheet capacity, Amazon may exit the cycle with even higher margins and stronger FCF, which the market currently discounts.

Thus, from my analysis and analytical point of view, I rate Amazon as a Buy, keeping a long-term time horizon of multiple years in mind.

data.title: Spirit AeroSystems: Boeing's Stock Slump Cuts Takeover Value To $37.25
data.description:
I covered Spirit AeroSystems (NYSE:SPR) in August with a "Hold" rating; since then, the stock has fallen 12.4%, which I attribute to the share price decline in Boeing’s (BA) stock price, as the acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems is bound to the share price performance of Boeing. In this report, I briefly discuss recent regulatory developments, the current fair value of Spirit AeroSystems stock based on Boeing’s acquisition offer, and what Boeing will be getting for that in return.

Approval From U.S. Regulator Still Pending

In June 2024, Boeing announced that it would be acquiring Spirit AeroSystems. The deal was expected to be completed by mid-2025. However, driven by delays in regulatory approval, the expected closing is now in the fourth quarter of 2025, subject to divestitures of certain business units and remaining regulatory hurdles. Shareholders of Spirit AeroSystems approved the acquisition in January 2025, but regulatory clearances were received much later than the mid-2025 target for completion of the acquisition. On June 30, the UK regulator launched a probe into Boeing’s acquisition of Spirit. Based on that probe, a decision would be made whether to launch a Phase 2 investigation, which would be even more time-consuming. However, in August, the decision was made not to initiate Phase 2, and that effectively meant a green light from the UK regulator. The EU antitrust approval followed on October 14, with U.S. approval pending at the time of writing.

Acquisition Price Falls As Boeing Stock Price Retreats

Boeing will be acquiring Spirit AeroSystems in an all-stock deal with the exchange rate depending on an exchange ratio that is dependent on Boeing’s stock price. When I wrote the article in August, Boeing’s stock price was $235.26, which would put the exchange ratio at 0.18, implying a $42.26 acquisition value and 2.7% upside. In hindsight, my upgrade to "Hold" was not the best call. As upside was limited, there were no substantial growth drivers for Boeing until the closing of the acquisition. However, there was a downside to Boeing’s stock price, which materialized as trade continued to be a topic, and there was a $4.9 billion charge on the Boeing 777X program in Q3. Boeing’s stock price fell to $195.50, which puts the stock's implied stock price at $37.25. Spirit’s current stock price is $36.14, implying around 3% upside.

Boeing Gets Control Over The Supply Chain

Spirit AeroSystems ended the quarter with $299 million in cash, $690.9 million in short-term debt, $8.2 million in short-term lease liabilities, $3.65 billion in long-term debt, and $58.9 million in long-term lease liabilities. This brings the debt position to $4.4 billion and the net debt position to $4.1 billion. The company has sold one of its facilities in Malaysia for $95.2 million, with the completion of the transaction expected this year. I expect a positive free cash flow in the fourth quarter of around $115 million.

Additionally, the company will pay Airbus to take some Airbus program work back. Since many of these programs are not making money, the commercial value of the work is zero, and the company has to pay Airbus $439 million plus $141.9 million in bridge funds provided by Airbus to Spirit AeroSystems, adjusted for $114 million in unpaid dues from Airbus. That brings the cash compensation at closing to $466.9 million. So, the pro forma debt related to the acquisition will be $3.8 billion, which is around $200 million higher than initially was expected.

What Boeing gets in return is not necessarily attractive in terms of financials. However, what is paramount for Boeing’s recovery path is that it gets better control of the supply chain to improve quality. Over the past decades, Boeing has transferred substantial risk to the supply chain, and quality has suffered not just from offloading the risk but also from friction between OEM and supplier responsibilities. With part of the supply base now coming back to Boeing, the company is eliminating that friction. It is eliminating some margins that Spirit used to have and thus were a cost for Boeing in the supplier-OEM relation, but that is really a negligible benefit weighed against the crucial quality control and management in the supply chain. For Boeing, it also means that in the future, on a next-generation airplane development, they can do more in-house, which means better quality control and harvesting more aftermarket sales opportunities.

Conclusion: The Final Steps For Spirit AeroSystems

With the acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems, Boeing is undoing a mistake it made by selling the company 20 years ago. The acquisition provides Boeing with better control and visibility in the supply chain, aiding its current key programs, but it will also aid the company in rolling out a next-generation airplane. For Boeing, it holds that it needs to integrate Spirit AeroSystems with discipline and quality as a core foundation rather than pushing the system to its edges and over it.

data.title: Realty Income Q3 Earnings: I'm Adding As Europe And Private Capital Will Serve As Tailwinds
data.description:
Realty Income (O) announced solid quarterly results last week, but its stock price remains quite low after recent decreases. Over the last couple of weeks, I had to watch O's stock price fall from over $60 per share to ~$57 currently.

With several positive news developments shared through this quarterly update and a more attractive entry point, I consider Realty Income to offer an even stronger buying opportunity than it did when I published my last article.

This is a follow-up coverage to my previous article when I rated O a "strong buy". Has my thesis changed and become bearish in light of the poor stock price performance?

On the contrary, I'm willing to state right from the get-go - I'm even more bullish on O, especially given its robust activity in Europe and a new private capital initiative. Yes, Realty Income puts a great deal of focus on Europe, but I think it will prove much more beneficial than many investors believe. Still, if you'd like to take a look at my previous articles about O - here's the link.

Without further ado, enjoy the read!

O is breaking the bank in Europe, but it may prove more beneficial than investors suspect

Despite my awareness that Realty Income has one of the broadest investment platforms in the U.S. real estate market with a substantial presence in Europe, its sourcing abilities continue to amaze me every year. In 2025 YTD, O was able to source as much as $97B of opportunity volume - I find this number truly overwhelming and signifying O's strength in the investment business, especially given how it has changed over the years.

Such a robust sourcing certainly helped O achieve investment volume recorded this quarter. During Q3 2025, O invested over $1.4B with a substantial part of investments realized in Europe - according to my previous expectations. Why does O favor Europe so much? I see that O continues to capitalize on quite attractive deal terms in Europe as compared to the U.S., which can be captured through both higher cap rates, lower cost of debt, and stronger negotiating position in a wider TAM.

As you can see above, O invested nearly $1B in Europe with "just" $380m invested in the U.S... speaking of the cap rate difference, it was quite a shock for me to see the difference amounting to as much as 100 bps (8% vs 7%).

Still, many investors are concerned with O's increasing activity in Europe given its less attractive positioning and growth prospects when compared to the U.S. However, I have to highlight that O is actually capturing great deals in Europe. Yes, yes - I know that judging by the cap rate itself is not enough to determine whether these deals actually add to its bottom line, but how can we verify that? Let's try to do that by calculating O's investment spread on these investments.

Based on O's updated AFFO per share guidance of $4.26 per share (at midpoint), I estimated its cost of equity at 7.5%. Based on not only O's credit rating, but also its own expectations - I assumed a cost of debt amounting to 4.9%. Still, keep in mind that O can secure cheaper debt in Europe, but I wouldn't like to make our assumptions too optimistic, would I? I'll stick to the number O shared within its Investor Presentation to remain on the safe side.

With all these assumptions, I arrived at a WACC of 6.6%. For a reference point, O estimated its WACC at 5.2% or 7%, depending on the approach.

With the 6.6% I calculated above, I can say that O's investment spread on its investments in Europe exceeds 1% and likely comes close to the 1.4%, as presented in the table above.

To conclude this section - I still see a lot of opportunities in Europe for O to explore. As long as it will be able to secure more attractive deal terms there versus in the U.S., I think these investment trends should continue. As management commented during the last Earnings Call:

The European investment opportunity continues to screen more favorably on a risk-adjusted basis relative to the U.S., which has become increasingly competitive from smaller platforms competing for similarly sized transactions.

In contrast, the European investment opportunity remains compelling, driven by a fragmented competitive landscape a larger total addressable market than the United States, and a current cost of debt for euro-denominated 10-year notes that is approximately 100 basis points inside of U.S. dollar costs.

(...)

If you look at the situation going forward, I would say that, that should continue. The majority of what we are seeing will continue to be in Europe, but we are starting to see some level of momentum here in the U.S. as well.

How private capital can boost O's effectiveness and create shareholder value?

Let me go through O's new "segment" - private capital that could be simplified as joint investments with Limited Partners (LPs) in exchange for a fee - 1% of LP NAV as suggested by O.

In business terms, it naturally makes sense for O to expand into that area of financial activity given its robust sourcing capabilities, wide investment platform with plenty of contacts in the investment world, as well as a highly respected brand it created for itself throughout the years that will ensure LPs interest.

But how does it look based on actual numbers? O shared a one-pager regarding that, but let's take a closer look to truly understand it.

Please keep in mind that these are illustration numbers, but as you can see, O expects the effective cap rate to climb by 260 bps from 7.5% to as much as 10.1%. Still, I understand that it may not be clear to all investors and some may wonder how Realty Income arrived at such a conclusion. That's why I decided to break it down for you and explain what drives the difference between 7.5% and 10.1%.

Hopefully this will help - I basically assumed an illustration scenario - investment amounting to a $100. With a basic model of O's activity, that would imply a 7.5% cap rate and O would finance the investment with equity (65%) and debt (35%) while taking 100% ownership.

In the second case, following "fund assumptions", O would only take 20% ownership with the rest to be taken by a Limited Partner (20 vs 80). In order to calculate LP's NAV, I deducted its "share in debt" amounting to 28 (80% x 35 debt value) from its ownership (80). That's how I calculated LP's NAV.

How do we arrive at 10.1% then? Finally, after taking into account O's inflows amounting to 1.5 (7.5% cap rate x 20 REIT ownership) and a fee from LP (1% of LP's NAV) amounting to 0.52, total inflows amounted to 2.02. If you divide these inflows by the REIT's ownership in the property (2.02 / 20), you will arrive at a 10.1% cap rate O suggested.

My conclusion: I'm even more bullish on O

I won't go on about Realty Income's stability and resilience to the market uncertainty, as I discussed this many times before. I found O's Q3 2025 update highly positive with several news items including improved investment guidance, robust investment activity in Europe, and a promising expansion into the private capital space.

I understand that the one-pager regarding private capital may be tricky to understand for some investors, but hopefully you found my example helpful.

I truly believe that O is securing highly attractive deals in Europe with several aspects to be considered - starting with a lower cost of debt, through more attractive cap rates, and a less competitive market. When it comes to private equity, it will certainly take considerable time before O reaches a meaningful scale with this initiative, but I think it will prove highly beneficial to its shareholders.

What does the valuation look like? With a weak stock price performance over the last couple of weeks, I find O's total return proposition even more promising. Its forward-looking P/FFO multiple currently sits at ~13.3x, which I consider too modest given O's growth prospects, business model, resilience, and a new private capital initiative.

Therefore, Realty Income remains a "strong buy" for me, but I'm happy to say that I'm even more bullish.

data.title: TSLW: Leveraged Tesla Strategy For High-Income Investors
data.description:
The Roundhill TSLA WeeklyPay ETF (TSLW) is a leveraged, actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with 1.2x the weekly total returns of Tesla (TSLA) shares in the form of income and growth. Unlike most leveraged strategies, TSLW was designed to be held long-term for income-seeking investors.

TSLW is a derivative strategy on TSLA shares. As a disclosure, I have a bullish recommendation on TSLA shares, largely driven by its robotaxi program and its electrification strategy.

You can read my most recent coverage of Tesla here:

“Tesla: Growth Will Be Supported By Accelerated Utility Capital Spend.”

About Roundhill TSLA WeeklyPay ETF

TSLW was launched by Roundhill Investments on February 19, 2025, on the Cboe BZX Exchange. The fund currently has $164mm in net assets, with an average of $11mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis. TSLW has a net expense ratio of 99bps, aligned with income-oriented single-stock strategies.

TSLW was designed to provide 1.2x the total return over the performance of TSLA shares, adding incremental value on a weekly basis. Performance can work both to the upside and downside risk in relation to TSLA shares, potentially amplifying gains or losses with respect to the underlying asset.

TSLW does this by employing weekly swap exposure on the performance of TSLA shares. As a result, the swaps will reset on a weekly basis in terms of performance. This is a core differentiator when considering leveraged funds, as many leveraged single-stock strategies like the Direxion Daily TSLA Bull 2x Shares ETF (TSLL) provide daily performance rather than weekly performance. This means that the fund will provide exposure to TSLA shares through the final day of the trading week, while taking into consideration bank holidays. This means that the fund will provide exposure through the end of the trading day on every Friday; if a bank holiday were to fall on a Friday, the fund would provide exposure to the prior closing date.

TSLW pays out a weekly distribution rate based on a number of factors, including the week’s total return of TSLA shares and the implied volatility of TSLA shares. As a result of this, weekly distributions can widely vary from week to week and may not be predictable. Investors should take into consideration that distributions are largely a return of capital, which may expose investors to certain tax implications. Strategies with a high return of capital are best held in taxable accounts in order to take full advantage of the tax deferment benefit. Return of capital will lower an investor’s cost basis until it reaches $0, in which case additional distributions will be taxed as ordinary income. As a result of this, investors may be exposed to a higher capital gains tax when selling out of TSLW shares.

Given TSLW’s inception date, we cannot determine the stability of TSLW’s distributions or long-term performance. On a forward basis, using TSLW’s most recent distribution rate, we can value TSLW’s forward distribution at $29.64/share at a forward yield of 85%.

TSLW has outperformed TSLA shares since inception, creating an appealing investment outlook relative to TSLA shares. TSLW’s portfolio is made up of weekly pay swaps with a portfolio weight of 94.52% and TSLA shares at roughly 25% of the portfolio weight, providing investors with roughly 120% exposure to TSLA shares. TSLW also holds short-term Treasury Bills as collateral against the swap positions.

Investor Suitability

TSLW can be utilized by a variety of investors seeking to amplify their exposure to TSLA shares. TSLW can be utilized by income investors seeking differentiated returns with respect to the performance of TSLA. TSLW can also be held for its relative total return performance relative to TSLA shares, particularly given its 1.2x weekly exposure to the stock.

Risks Related To TSLW

Given the leverage component of the strategy, TSLW may expose investors to heightened risk relative to TSLA shares. TSLW targets 1.2x the performance of TSLA shares, exposing investors to both the upside and downside risk. TSLW may experience a weekly performance reset as a result of the weekly swap positions, potentially resulting in a compounding effect when held for longer periods of time. If TSLA shares were to experience a significant downturn, TSLW may amplify losses and potentially return a lower distribution rate.

Investors must take into consideration the tax and fee implications before investing in TSLW. Return of capital may impact an investor’s tax burden, particularly when selling out of a position with a lower cost basis relative to the initial purchase of shares. Actively trading TSLW may expose investors to short-term capital gains taxes in addition to the portfolio fees.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

TSLW can be an appealing strategy for investors seeking amplified returns on TSLA shares. TSLW can provide investors with both growth and income as part of its leveraged investment strategy, and may outperform TSLA over time. With the weekly performance reset, investors must be cautious about the potential compounding effect when holding TSLW for longer than a single week; however, the compounding effect may not be as drastic as ETFs that invest in swap derivatives that are reset on a daily basis. Given the income and growth components of the strategy, I am recommending TSLW with a Buy rating.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Takes Flight
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 6.4 percentage points to 31.6%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the third time in five weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 6.5 percentage points to 19.2%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 69th time in 71 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 12.9 percentage points to 49.1%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 50th time in 52 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 19.2 percentage points to –17.5%. The bull-bear spread is unusually low and is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 37th time in 41 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what impact they think the government shutdown is having on the economy.

Here is how they responded:

  • It’s slowing the economy: 55.8%
  • The impact is limited: 19.5%
  • It’s not having much of an impact yet, but that could change: 18.7%
  • It’s not impacting the economy: 3.6%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 2.4%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 31.6%, down 6.4 percentage points
  • Neutral: 19.2%, down 6.5 percentage points
  • Bearish: 49.1%, up 12.9 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.
